policy_statement: Retrofit 20% of total 5+ unit residential buildings by 2030.
mechanism_description: Retrofitting residential buildings improves efficiency of building envelopes and systems, lowering energy consumption and associated emissions.
secondary_justification: Resource Efficiency: Efficiency retrofits deliver the same household energy services (comfort, hot water, lighting) with less energy input, i.e., reduced energy intensity as a direct physical consequence of improved building systems/envelope.
primary_causal_pathway: Building operation (retrofit upgrades) → ↓ energy demand (kWh/therms) → ↓ fuel combustion / ↓ grid generation → ↓ GHG emissions → ↓ atmospheric forcing
causal_mechanism_detail:
(1) Change in human activity/system: Building owners/occupants implement retrofits in existing homes (e.g., insulation/air sealing, window upgrades, HVAC replacements, heat pumps, efficient lighting/appliances, controls/commissioning).  
(2) Mediating physical process: Improved thermal resistance and reduced air leakage lower heating/cooling loads; higher equipment efficiencies and better controls reduce energy required to provide the same end-use services.  
(3) Climate-relevant outcome: Lower site and source energy use reduces combustion of fossil fuels (directly in buildings and indirectly in power generation), cutting CO₂ (and in some cases methane from fuel supply chains) emissions.
dominant_pathway_test: The single biggest climate-relevant effect is reduced greenhouse gas emissions from lower residential energy consumption (and associated reduced fuel combustion in buildings and the electricity grid).
mechanism_classification_reasoning:
1) Dominant causal pathway: Residential retrofits primarily reduce energy consumption, which reduces upstream/downstream fuel combustion and thus GHG emissions.  
2) Why this primary over alternatives: While retrofits can also improve comfort and resilience, the mechanism as stated (“energy_use_reduction”) is directly tied to emissions reduction, making Mitigation the dominant climate effect across contexts.  
3) Inherent causal chain for each secondary:  
   - Resource Efficiency: Retrofits increase energy productivity (same service with less energy), which is inherently true whenever energy use is reduced via efficiency measures.  
4) Edge cases applied: If a retrofit is purely for electrification without reducing energy (or if rebound effects negate savings), the mechanism would differ; however, this canonical mechanism explicitly centers on energy reduction, so mitigation remains primary.  
5) Confirm labels apply to ALL instances: Any policy that retrofits homes to reduce energy use will (all else equal) reduce energy demand and associated emissions, and will inherently improve energy efficiency/resource use per unit of service.
mechanism_edge_case_notes: Rebound effects (increased energy use due to improved affordability/comfort) can erode savings; grid decarbonization reduces marginal GHG benefits over time; some “retrofits” may focus on electrification or resilience (e.g., storm hardening) rather than energy reduction—those would warrant a different mechanism/category, but are outside this specified mechanism.
co_benefits: Potential co-benefits include improved indoor comfort/health, reduced energy bills, and potential resilience to temperature extremes via better building envelopes, but these are not stated and no hazard context is provided to justify an additional secondary category.
instance_edge_case_notes: Instrument could be interpreted as a mandate if embedded in enforceable requirements, but as written it reads as a standalone retrofit target without explicit compliance/enforcement language.
